metadata.title: Sieve of Erato67henes
metadata.description:
time limit per test
1 second
memory limit per test
256 megabytes
input
standard input
output
standard output

You are given $$$n$$$ positive integers $$$a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n$$$.
Please determine if it is possible to select any number of elements in $$$a$$$, so that their product is $$$67$$$.
Note that you may not select zero elements, as the product of zero elements is not defined in this problem.

Input
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10^4$$$). The description of the test cases follows. 
The first line of each test case contains a single integer $$$n$$$ ($$$1 \le n \le 5$$$).
The second line of each test case contains $$$n$$$ positive integers $$$a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n$$$ ($$$1 \le a_i \le 67$$$).

Output
If it is possible to select elements so that their product is $$$67$$$, output "YES" on one line. Otherwise, output "NO" on one line.
You can output the answer in any case. For example, the strings "yEs", "yes", and "Yes" will also be recognized as positive responses.

metadata.title: Glasses Off
metadata.description:
time limit per test
8 seconds
memory limit per test
256 megabytes
input
standard input
output
standard output

Today's contest is a team contest! But why limit yourself to groups of three? Well, for starters, if you were seen with more than three people in your group, you would be disqualified from the contest! So, you have two choices: either work in groups of three, or make it so Mark, the ACPC officer in the room, can't see you break this rule.
You've chosen the latter, and through a clever plan, have stolen Mark's glasses! Now, for Mark, everything will be a blur! With Mark's eyesight nerfed, all that's left is to make sure that you and your teammates don't stand out.
Notably, the visually-impaired Mark will be able to pick out the colors of each contest participant's shirts, and will pay attention to the color(s) that appear most frequently in the contest room. As a result, if you or any of your friends wear a shirt that is the same color as any of the most frequently appearing colors, then you risk getting noticed!
You're at Kohl's now and are ready to buy the shirts needed. There are $$$N$$$ colors, and you know how many participants in the contest room are wearing each color. Given this information, how many people (including you) can you sneak into the contest without getting caught?

Input
The first line contains $$$N$$$ ($$$1 \le N \le 10^5$$$), the number of different shirt colors offered by Kohl's.
The next line contains N space-separated integers $$$x_i$$$ ($$$0 \le x_i \le 1000$$$), denoting the number of people in the contest room wearing the $$$i^{th}$$$ color.

Output
Output one integer: the maximum number of people you can sneak into the contest room without getting noticed.

metadata.title: Monolingual Nightmare
metadata.description:
time limit per test
1 second
memory limit per test
256 megabytes
input
standard input
output
standard output

It was almost so perfect! You uncovered the secret location where the ACPC problemsetters meet. To listen in on the meetings, you snuck a parrot into the problemsetters' meeting room. Now, the parrot can listen in on the meeting and recite what the problemsetters say right back to you. It's a flawless plan – except for the fact that this parrot only speaks Spanish.
Your parrot has a flawless memory and will remember every word that the problemsetters say in order. However, since the problemsetters speak in a different language than the Parrot, the parrot will only remember the words that sound similar to words that it knows in Spanish. All other words will be unrecognizable and replaced with a "???".
The meeting has concluded and the parrot has fully recited the conversation to you, in Spanish. Now, it's up to you to determine what the problemsetters could have said in English! As a result, for many candidate English conversations, which ones are possible conversations the problemsetters could have had?

Input
The first line contains integers $$$N$$$, $$$L$$$, and $$$Q$$$ ($$$1 \le N, L, Q \le 100$$$): the number of words in the parrot's dictionary, the length of the conversation in words, and the number of queries.
The next $$$N$$$ lines follow the format "$$$a$$$ <=> $$$b$$$", where $$$a$$$ is an English word and $$$b$$$ is a Spanish word which sounds similar. No word will appear will appear twice in this dictionary, and no English word will also be a Spanish word (or visa versa).
Then, a blank line.
The next line contains $$$L$$$ words: the conversation the parrot recited, in Spanish. Unrecognized words will be labeled with a "???".
Then, a blank line.
The next $$$Q$$$ lines each contain one candidate English conversation, each also of length $$$L$$$.

Output
For each candidate English conversation, output "YES" or "NO" if it is possible for the problemsetters to have spoken that conversation.

metadata.title: Floor Plan
metadata.description:
time limit per test
2 seconds
memory limit per test
256 megabytes
input
standard input
output
standard output

The ideal team strategy is to have one person coding, one person thinking about other problems, and one person walking around the competition room to look at other teams' computers and gather intel. You are planning the perfect way for your teammate to walk around the room for said intel, but are missing a key piece of information: the dimensions of the room!
Let the room be represented by a $$$N$$$x$$$M$$$ grid, where $$$N$$$ and $$$M$$$ are unknown. In this room, there exist empty spaces '.' and tables 'T'. Your goal is to determine all possible values for $$$N$$$ and $$$M$$$.
To do this, you will use two details you've gathered about the room:
(1) No two tables will touch, even diagonally.
(2) You found a document which contains the row-major traversal of the room, represented as a string of '.' and 'T'.
So, given the row-major traversal, can you find all possible values for $$$N$$$ and $$$M$$$?

Input
The first line contains one string of length $$$N * M$$$ ($$$1 \le N * M \le 10^5$$$); the row-major traversal of the room.

Output
Begin the output with one integer $$$X$$$: the number of potential dimensions for the room.
Then, for the next $$$X$$$ lines, output a potential dimension of the room, in the form "$$$N$$$x$$$M$$$". Here, $$$N$$$ is the number of rows and $$$M$$$ is the number of columns.
You may output the possible room dimensions in any order.

metadata.title: Challenging Encounter
metadata.description:
time limit per test
1 second
memory limit per test
256 megabytes
input
standard input
output
standard output

Steve was so busy reorganizing his inventory that he never got around to building shelter! As the first day draws to a close, a pack of zombies lurches towards him from the shadows. 
Steve has $$$X$$$ health and is fighting $$$N$$$ zombies, each having $$$A$$$ health. When he hits a zombie, he deals $$$Y$$$ damage to the zombie reducing its health by $$$Y$$$, and the zombie hits back for $$$B$$$ damage reducing Steve's health by $$$B$$$. 
If when attacking a zombie its health drops to $$$0$$$ or below, it does not hit back and instead drops a rotten flesh that Steve can choose to consume immediately to gain $$$Z$$$ health. If Steve's health drops to $$$0$$$ or below, then he dies. 
Steve's health is capped to $$$X$$$. If his health is currently $$$x$$$ and he consumes a rotten flesh, his health will become $$$\text{min}(X, x + Z)$$$. Also note that he cannot wait to consume a rotten flesh; he must consume it immediately after killing a zombie or not at all. 
Is it possible for Steve to kill all the zombies and survive the night?

Input
The first and only line will contain six integers $$$N$$$, $$$A$$$, $$$B$$$, $$$X$$$, $$$Y$$$, and $$$Z$$$ $$$(1 \le N, A, B, X, Y, Z \le 10^9)$$$, where:
$$$N$$$ is the number of zombies, $$$A$$$ is the health of each zombie, $$$B$$$ is the damage a zombie deals back each time Steve hits it and the zombie survives, $$$X$$$ is Steve's maximum and starting health, $$$Y$$$ is the damage Steve deals with each hit, $$$Z$$$ is the amount of health Steve recovers by consuming a rotten flesh. 
Output
For each test case, print YES if it is possible for Steve to kill all $$$N$$$ zombies and survive, and NO otherwise.
You may print each letter in any case. For example, yes, Yes, and YES will all be accepted.

metadata.title: Kitties
metadata.description:
time limit per test
1 second
memory limit per test
256 megabytes
input
standard input
output
standard output

While traversing through the jungle, Steve comes across some ocelots! They're amazing pets and very cute, so he stops to tame them before going to the stronghold. 
Steve has constructed a map showing where all the fish and ocelots in the jungle are. This map is given to you as a string of length $$$N$$$ consisting of the characters 'F', indicating a fish, and 'O', indicating an ocelot. 
Steve starts at index $$$1$$$. Each day, he may move to an adjacent index, while remaining within the bounds of the string. Formally, if Steve is currently at index $$$i$$$, then in one day he may move to an index $$$j$$$ such that $$$ |i - j| = 1$$$ and $$$1 \le j \le N.$$$ Steve is allowed to revisit indices any number of times.
If Steve is at the same index as a fish, then Steve can add one fish to his inventory, removing the fish from the map. If Steve is at the same index as an ocelot and has at least one fish in his inventory, he can use one fish from his inventory to tame the ocelot (consuming the fish) and removing the ocelot from the map. Steve cannot obtain multiple fish from one 'F' index, nor can he tame multiple ocelots from one 'O' index. 
Given that Steve starts at index $$$1$$$ with no fish in his inventory, help him figure out the minimum number of days it will take to tame all of the ocelots and return to index $$$1$$$, or report that it's impossible.

Input
The first line contains a single integer $$$N$$$ ($$$1 \le N \le 2 \cdot 10^5$$$), the length of the string.
The second line contains a string $$$S$$$ of length $$$N$$$, consisting only of characters 'F' and 'O'.

Output
Print the minimum number of days Steve needs to tame all ocelots and return home, or $$$-1$$$ if it is impossible.

metadata.title: Exploding Houses
metadata.description:
time limit per test
1 second
memory limit per test
256 megabytes
input
standard input
output
standard output

After finding the village, he realizes that all the villager houses are already looted by his friends! In a fit of anger, Steve resolves to destroy all his friends' houses, and places TNT right beneath every single one.
Steve has place $$$N$$$ blocks of TNT numbered from $$$1$$$ to $$$N$$$. He has also written down a list of $$$M$$$ pairs indicating which TNT blocks are adjacent. 
A TNT block may be manually ignited at any integer time $$$t$$$. If a TNT block is ignited at time $$$t$$$, then it explodes at time $$$t+1$$$. When a TNT block explodes, every TNT block adjacent to it becomes ignited at the time of the explosion. Once a TNT block has exploded, it cannot be ignited or used again.
Unfortunately, Steve's TNT is unstable. If two adjacent TNT blocks explode at the same time, the server will crash. In other words, for every pair of adjacent TNT blocks, their explosion times must be different.
Help Steve figure out the minimum number of manual ignitions required to blow up all $$$N$$$ TNT blocks without a server crash, or tell him that it is impossible to do so. 

Input
The first line will contain two integers $$$N, M$$$, $$$(1 \leq N \leq 10^5)$$$, $$$(0 \leq M \leq 10^5)$$$, denoting the number of TNT and the number of adjacent TNT pairs respectively. 
The following $$$M$$$ lines will each contain two integers $$$(i, j)$$$ $$$(1 \leq i, j \leq N)$$$, indicating that TNT $$$i$$$ is adjacent to TNT $$$j$$$. 

Output
Output the minimum number of manual ignitions to blow up all TNT blocks without a server crash, or $$$0$$$ if it is impossible to do so.

metadata.title: Exploding Houses
metadata.description:
time limit per test
1 second
memory limit per test
256 megabytes
input
standard input
output
standard output

After finding the village, he realizes that all the villager houses are already looted by his friends! In a fit of anger, Steve resolves to destroy all his friends' houses, and places TNT right beneath every single one.
Steve has place $$$N$$$ blocks of TNT numbered from $$$1$$$ to $$$N$$$. He has also written down a list of $$$M$$$ pairs indicating which TNT blocks are adjacent. 
A TNT block may be manually ignited at any integer time $$$t$$$. If a TNT block is ignited at time $$$t$$$, then it explodes at time $$$t+1$$$. When a TNT block explodes, every TNT block adjacent to it becomes ignited at the time of the explosion. Once a TNT block has exploded, it cannot be ignited or used again.
Unfortunately, Steve's TNT is unstable. If two adjacent TNT blocks explode at the same time, the server will crash. In other words, for every pair of adjacent TNT blocks, their explosion times must be different.
Help Steve figure out the minimum number of manual ignitions required to blow up all $$$N$$$ TNT blocks without a server crash, or tell him that it is impossible to do so.

metadata.title: Greedy Piglins
metadata.description:
time limit per test
1 second
memory limit per test
256 megabytes
input
standard input
output
standard output

A caravan of piglins clatters through the Nether, hauling chests and sacks stuffed with all sorts of treasure. As Steve watches the commotion, he spots a few precious ender pearls being traded from piglin to piglin. 
Steve has run into a group of $$$N$$$ piglins with a rather unusual system for trading. The $$$N$$$ piglins form a line, starting from piglin $$$1$$$ and ending with piglin $$$N$$$. A trade performed with the $$$i$$$th piglin requires $$$T_i$$$ seconds and has probability $$$P_i$$$ of succeeding. If you are at piglin $$$i$$$ and the trade succeeds, then you should move to piglin $$$i + 1$$$. If the trade fails, then you instead move backwards to piglin $$$F_i$$$ where $$$F_i \leq i$$$.
According to ancient custom, Steve must start trading with piglin $$$1$$$ and cannot stop until he's made a successful trade with piglin $$$N$$$. Steve wonders what is the expected amount of seconds he will be stuck trading. 
It can be shown that the expected number of seconds can be represented as an irreducible fraction $$$\frac{a}{b}$$$ where $$$b \not \equiv 0 \pmod{10^9 + 7}$$$. Output the value $$$a \cdot b^{-1} \pmod{10^9 + 7}$$$ where $$$b^{-1}$$$ denotes the modular inverse of $$$b$$$ modulo $$$10^9 + 7$$$. 

Input
The first line contains a single integer $$$N$$$ $$$(1 \le N \le 10^5)$$$.
The next $$$N$$$ lines describe the piglins. The $$$i$$$th of these lines contains three integers $$$T_i$$$, $$$Q_i$$$, and $$$F_i$$$ $$$(1 \le T_i \le 10^9,\ 1 \le Q_i \le 100,\ 1 \le F_i \le i)$$$, where:
$$$T_i$$$ is the number of seconds required to trade with piglin $$$i$$$, $$$Q_i$$$ is the success probability of trading with piglin $$$i$$$ expressed as a percentage, so the true probability $$$P_i$$$ is $$$\frac{Q_i}{100}$$$, $$$F_i$$$ is the piglin you move to if the trade with piglin $$$i$$$ fails. 
Output
Print a single integer, the expected number of seconds required to make a successful trade with piglin $$$N$$$, assuming you start at piglin $$$1$$$, modulo $$$10^9+7$$$.
